text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the retrocecal recess and performs inferior to superior mobilization of the ascending colon, exposing an avascular plane along Told's line to reveal the duodenum and right kidney. Dissection continues to the subhepatic area, followed by lateral mobilization and primary vascular control with D-III lymphadenectomy along the right border of the superior mesenteric vessels. Near-infrared fluorescence imaging is used for lymphatic mapping, although lymphatic flow is not identified, leading to lymph node dissection along the central vascular trunk.

Intravenous injection of five milligrams of endocyanine green dye is administered, and near-infrared fluorescence imaging reveals the vascular anatomy near the ileocolic vessels. The ileocolic artery and vein are visualized, with the vein ligated using hemo-lock clips and transected at its root, followed by the artery. INIF imaging is used to identify and dissect suspicious remnant lymph node tissue around the ligated ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon transects the greater omentum of the transverse colon and detaches the hepatic flexure. The transverse mesocolon is divided using the endo-wrist-I vessel sealer to prepare for anastomosis. After intravenous injection of ICG dye, INIF imaging is used to assess bowel perfusion, and the optimal division point is selected. The transverse colon is transected with the endo-wrist stapler, which is then used to create a side-to-side anastomosis in an isoparastaltic manner. The stapler insertion site is closed with continuous stitches using V-lock sutures.
